intitule: Lesson 14: Expressing Future Plans - Future Tenses (will vs. be going to)

enonce.texte:
Grammar Resource: Expressing Future Plans

In English, we use different forms to talk about the future. The two most common for expressing plans and intentions are 'will' and 'be going to'.

  • will + verb: Used for spontaneous decisions, promises, offers, and predictions based on opinion.
  • be going to + verb: Used for pre-meditated plans, intentions, and predictions based on present evidence.

Examples:

  • "I have decided. I am going to become a journalist." (Plan/Intention)
  • "The phone is ringing. I will answer it." (Spontaneous decision)
  • "Look at those dark clouds. It is going to rain." (Prediction based on evidence)
  • "I promise I will study harder next year." (Promise)

Complete the following exercises to master the difference.

questions.texte: Identify whether the following sentences express a 'plan/intention' (use 'be going to') or a 'spontaneous decision/promise' (use 'will'). Then, complete the sentence with the correct form of the verb in brackets.

sousQuestions.texte: After class, I ________ (call) my parents to tell them about my career choice. I've been thinking about it for weeks.
Don't worry about the application fee. I ________ (lend) you the money.
She has already bought a CV folder and a new suit. She ________ (apply) for the manager position.

questions.texte: Differentiate between the uses of 'will' and 'be going to' by choosing the correct option to complete each sentence below.

sousQuestions.texte: Based on his excellent grades, he ________ (is going to succeed / will succeed) in his entrance exam.
A: "I don't understand this lesson." B: "I ________ (am going to help / will help) you right now."
They have already made a budget and saved money. They ________ (will travel / are going to travel) abroad next year.

intitule: Lesson 14: Writing a Persuasive Letter about a Career Choice

enonce.texte:
Real-Life Problem: Persuading Your Family

You are a student in Terminale A in Cameroon. You have decided to pursue a career in journalism because you want to inform people and fight against injustice. However, your parents want you to become a doctor because they believe it is a more respectable and secure job. They have told you they will not pay for university if you choose journalism.

Your task: Write a formal letter to your parents to explain your choice, persuade them of its merits, and express your future plans using the appropriate future tenses.

Length: 250-300 words.
images: A young Cameroonian student sitting at a desk, holding a pen, with a thoughtful expression, surrounded by books and a notebook. A speech bubble above shows a newspaper and a stethoscope, symbolizing a career choice dilemma between journalism and medicine.
An opened letter on a wooden table in a living room setting, with a pen beside it. The letter has a clear heading 'Dear Parents' and visible paragraphs.

questions.texte: Write a persuasive letter to your parents. In your letter, you must: (a) State your career plan clearly using 'be going to' or 'will'. (b) Give at least three reasons for your choice (passion, skills, societal impact). (c) Address their concerns about respect and security. (d) Make a promise about your future success using 'will'. (e) Use appropriate linking words (because, therefore, however, although).
questions.reponse: See the detailed rubric below for the expected answer structure and content.

intitule: Lesson 14: Speaking - Role-Playing a Career Discussion

enonce.texte:
Real-Life Problem: Justifying Your Choice in a Dialogue

Work with a partner. One of you will be the student (who wants to be a journalist), and the other will be the parent (who wants the child to be a doctor).

Situation: The student has just received the university admission letter. The parent is angry because the student applied for journalism school secretly. They have a conversation at home.
images: A living room scene with two chairs facing each other. One person is a young adult holding a letter, looking determined. The other is an older adult with a crossed-arm posture, looking disappointed. A university brochure is on the table.
A dialogue bubble showing key phrases: 'I am going to explain my decision...', 'But I promise I will...', 'If you become a doctor, you will...', 'I have planned to...'

questions.texte: Create a 2-3 minute dialogue where the student defends their career choice using 'be going to' (for plans) and 'will' (for promises and spontaneous responses). The parent should express worries and counter-arguments. Use the following prompts to structure your dialogue.
questions.reponse: See the example dialogue below for a model answer.

sousQuestions.texte: Student: State your plan and one reason (passion). Use 'be going to'.
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: 'I am going to study journalism because I am passionate about telling the truth.'

sousQuestions.texte: Parent: Express a worry (job security). Use 'will' for a prediction.
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: 'If you become a journalist, you will struggle to find a stable job.'

sousQuestions.texte: Student: Respond spontaneously and make a promise. Use 'will'.
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: 'I understand your worry, but I promise I will work hard and create my own opportunities.'

sousQuestions.texte: Parent: Conclude by stating a condition for their support.
sousQuestions.reponse: Example: 'Alright, but you will have to find an internship every holiday. That is the condition.'
